summary: Shaw shares his practice session with watercolor and ink, explaining the fundamental challenge of watercolor: it's transparent, so you paint light to dark using the paper as white. He walks through a simple three-step process for building watercolor paintings using value layers, starting with the lightest wash and progressively adding darker tones. He experiments with adding pen and ink to color watercolors but remains unconvinced about combining the two media.
key_points: Watercolor is the inverse of opaque paints because you build from light to dark, using paper as your white
Water control on the paper vs. brush determines edge quality — wet paper gives soft edges, dry paper gives hard edges
A three-step value layering process (white, light gray, mid-gray, then darks) provides a systematic approach to watercolor
Practicing with monochrome ink and water is the easiest way to learn watercolor fundamentals before adding color
money_quotes: Watercolor is the inverse of other paints because it's transparent so you build paintings from light to dark, using the paper as your white "pigment".
The easiest way to get used to this is to paint with just ink and water or any transparent black paint.
The advantage of watercolor is you can control the edges on these shapes if you dry the paper or not between each layer of paint.
If you have more water then the edges will be soft and out of focus. If you dry it then the edges will be hard and in focus.
Do these kind of monochrome paintings for a while and you'll get the hang of water as an edge control and using layers of watercolor to build the painting.
Those are birds or mosquitos or something annoying and tiny.
I really like ink for drawing and a brush pen to do monochrome paintings, but still not sure about it in watercolors.
key_insight: Watercolor painting becomes approachable when you practice with monochrome ink first and think systematically about building value layers from light to dark.
